data.title: Replimune: Further Uncertainty Of RP1 Warrants Move To 'Hold' Rating
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Replimune (NASDAQ:REPL) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Replimune: Scope Goes Beyond That of RP1 Oncolytic Targeting For Melanoma." With respect to this article, I noted the company's opportunity to potentially receive Accelerated Approval of its therapy RP1 in combination with Opdivo [nivolumab] for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma. As an update since that article, the company did not receive U.S. marketing approval of RP1.

As a matter of fact, it received a complete response letter [CRL] for its Biologics License Application [BLA] of this therapy for the treatment of these advanced melanoma patients. Furthermore, the company requested a Type A meeting with the FDA as well to discuss the CRL of its RP1 therapy combination for the treatment of these patients. The hope is that there would be some type of clarity obtained after such a meeting. However, the truth is that there was no progress made at all. The company received some type of feedback, but it did not receive any clarity on whether or not there still might be a path forward for the advancement of RP1 under an Accelerated Approval pathway for U.S. marketing.

My inclination is to move this from a "Strong Buy" to a "Hold" rating for this stock. The reason why I'm not moving right away to a "Sell" rating is because no response was given in terms of what the next steps are. Once the company meets with the FDA again and defines what possible next steps it will take with respect to the development of RP1 + Opdivo for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma, then I would likely change the rating again. I believe that if the company folds shop and chooses the strategic evaluation route for its entire pipeline, then that would warrant a "Sell" rating.

RP1 Appeared To Be Good On Move Towards Possible Accelerated Approval For Advanced Melanoma

As I noted above, Replimune announced that it had received a complete response letter [CRL] of its Biologics License Application [BLA] of RP1 plus Opdivo for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma. The thing is that the FDA had found three problems with respect to the BLA that was submitted. The first and most notable issue raised in the CRL was that IGNYTE was not a well-controlled trial. In other words, the FDA didn't believe that the data was convincing enough. The truth is that the RP1 combination did particularly well, establishing a confirmed objective response rate [cORR] of 32.9%. Along with a duration of response [DOR] of 33.7 months. The gripe here is that the trial that was done wasn't a randomized, placebo-controlled type. One valid point raised by the company is that in prior interactions with the FDA, it was never noted that the IGNYTE trial alone would not be enough to warrant U.S. marketing approval under an Accelerated Approval pathway. Specifically, mid-stage and late-stage cycle reviews didn't mention this issue. Another problem highlighted in the CRL was the heterogeneity of the patient population.

With the company receiving this CRL from the FDA for RP1 + Opdivo for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma, it quickly moved to request a Type A meeting with the FDA. What was the whole point of this move? The need for this was to obtain feedback and to see if there would somehow still be a chance to have RP1 approved based on the Accelerated Approval pathway. Unfortunately, this did not materialize at all. Especially since after the conclusion of this Type A meeting, this U.S. agency feedback was obtained, but no path of possible Accelerated Approval pathway had been determined. The reason why I'm moving the stock to a "Hold Rating" is because there was no clear answer of whether or not a quicker pathway for approval would still be possible for RP1 plus Opdivo to treat these patients with advanced melanoma.

Thus, I believe there is hope that in the next several months there could be further clarity on what Replimune wants to do as it relates to this specific candidate going forward. That is, there are likely three scenarios that might play out here. One option could be a pipeline reprioritization of RP1 or RP2 to target other types of solid tumors. For instance, it is currently evaluating the use of RP2 in a phase 2 study in combination with 2nd-line or 3rd-line drugs to treat patients with locally advanced unresectable or metastatic liver cancer. A second option would be what I briefly mentioned above, which would be to enact strategic alternatives like a merger or outright sale of the company itself. The third and final possible item might be that the FDA could still give it guidance in terms of pressing on with a BLA of RP1 under an Accelerated Approval pathway. This last option, though, is likely to be a long shot and is not guaranteed.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Replimune had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $403.3 million as of June 30, 2025. The truth is that this biotech doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, and it is my belief that it is likely going to have to raise additional cash soon enough. Why do I make that particular claim? It is because in its 10-Q SEC filing, it notes that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q4 of 2026. It is not going to wait until next year to start looking at ways to raise funds. I think a cash raise could be done as early as the beginning of 2026. The company burns roughly $90.4 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $57.8 million in R&D expenses and then $32.6 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Replimune. The first risk to consider would be both the receiving of the Complete Response Letter [CRL] and the conclusion of the Type A meeting. The CRL was a huge crushing blow to this company, and the hope was that the Type A meeting with the FDA was going to help it obtain clarity on a path forward for RP1 plus Opdivo for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma. The risk here is that upon additional meetings with the FDA, there is no assurance that an Accelerated Approval pathway will be attainable. Without such a pathway being in place, then that would force the company to possibly move forward with the confirmatory trial. This would result in an extended timeline to potentially receive U.S. marketing approval of RP1 for the treatment of these specific melanoma patients.

The second risk to consider would be an option that Replimune might be forced to take. With no quick pathway towards approval of RP1, it might move to one of its other options. For instance, it could choose to reprioritize its pipeline or enact strategic alternatives like a merger. The risk here is that even if the company takes one of these alternative steps, there is no assurance that it won't run into any other issues going forward. The point here is that there is a risk that the company will continue to operate as a business.

A third risk to consider would be its fallback option I laid out above. This would be in terms of its other oncolytic program of RP2 Engineered HSV backbone, GM-CSF, GALV-GP R-, anti-CTLA-4 in combination with 1st or 2nd-line therapy for the treatment of patients with locally advanced or unresectable or metastatic liver cancer. This particular program is being explored in an ongoing phase 2 study targeting this group of patients. The hope is that the company will be able to shift to this program if the RP1 program targeting melanoma patients falters. The risk here is that upon completion of this mid-stage trial, there is no guarantee that positive results will be released from it. Nor that the data to be produced from this phase 2 study will be enough to warrant further advancement.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that Replimune finds itself in. The reason why is because it goes back to what I noted above in the financial section, which is that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q4 of 2026. The risk here is that the company is likely going to need to raise additional cash using some type of financial tool at its disposal in the next several months. The risk is that there is no assurance that it is going to be able to obtain adequate funding to keep itself and its pipeline funded for starters. Secondly, even if it does obtain some type of financing to fund itself, there is no guarantee that it will be on acceptable terms.

Conclusion

Replimune may have received a Complete Response Letter [CRL] from the FDA for RP1 + Opdivo for the treatment of patients with advanced melanoma. However, I believe that it is important to move the stock to a "Hold" rating. The reason why is because despite a minuscule chance, it is possible that further dialogue with the FDA may yield an Accelerated Approval pathway going forward. However, I don't believe that it is out of the realm of possibility of it either moving towards the use of RP2 to target patients with liver cancer instead. In addition, it has other options in place, like reprioritizing its pipeline or enacting strategic alternatives. With the possibility of an accelerated pathway forward for RP1 + Opdivo for advanced melanoma, plus the ability to continue to expand the use of either RP1 or RP2 for other solid tumor cancer indications, I believe that investors could possibly benefit from some gains made here.

data.title: CIK: Interest Rates May Be A Growth Catalyst, But Not A Clear Buy Yet
data.description:
Overview

The Fed has just implemented a 25 basis point cut to interest rates, and I believe that this will increase investor sentiment around income funds. A lower interest rate means that credit funds will now see healthier operating margins and may be able to provide better growth going forward. Credit Suisse Asset Management Income Fund (NYSE:CIK) aims to provide attractive total return from its portfolio of high-yield income investments, and lower interest rates may be a catalyst. When I previously covered CIK, I issued a hold rating due to the lackluster NAV growth.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CIK's share price has declined by about 4.2%. Despite riding the positive momentum of the market rally over the last few months, the share price is still down from last year. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to roughly 5.8% over the same time frame. CIK now offers investors a generous dividend yield slightly over 9% and pays out on a monthly basis. This makes CIK an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income generation.

Since the time of my last coverage, CIK has released an updated semi-annual report. This prompted me to revisit the fund to provide some refreshed insights into its performance and outlook for the remainder of the year. While the NAV has trended upwards over the last few years, I still think there are some areas where the fund can improve. For instance, I would love to see net investment income levels fully support distribution on their own, so that the fund can be more resilient to market downturns. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that CIK implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the fact sheet, CIK has total net assets of $160.6M that are spread across 236 different holdings. The fund's primary objective is to generate current income for shareholders. The way CIK achieves this is by investing in a blend of different fixed income and debt securities, which may be structured on a floating rate basis. This enables CIK to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment by generating elevated levels of interest income from its securities. The fund leans most heavily on high yield bonds, but the breakdown of the portfolio by asset is as follows:

  • High Yield Bonds: 79.49%
  • Senior Loans: 14.38%
  • Asset-Backed Securities: 5.24%
  • Equities: 0.51%

Expanding on this diversity, CIK also allocates its assets across a diverse range of industries as a way to mitigate concentration risks. As we can see below, CIK is most heavily allocated to Basic Industry at 17.65% of assets. For context, 'Basic Industry' generally refers to businesses that are related to the production or processing of raw materials. This is followed by exposure to Technology and Capital Goods securities, which account for 12.91% and 12.29% of assets, respectively. The combination of these three industries makes up over 42.8% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these have the greatest impact on CIK's daily price movement and total return potential.

Looking at the holdings below, we can see a unique blend between public and private investments. For instance, CIK provides exposure to public investments such as Northern Oil and Gas (NOG) and Insight Enterprises (NSIT). Despite the wide range of exposure to different holdings, I want to emphasize that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time, since CIK is focused primarily on income generation.

To demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance chart of CIK over the last decade. As we can see below, CIK's share price has only increased by about 3.1% over the last ten years. Therefore, we should not expect CIK to be an efficient way to participate in market rallies. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return sits around 149% over the same period. Therefore, investors should anticipate that their returns will primarily come from the fund's ability to continue paying out large distributions. On a more positive note, CIK has demonstrated that it can preserve investor capital over a longer period of time.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my prior coverage, CIK traded at a small discount to NAV of approximately 1.4%. Following the recent market rally, CIK now trades at a small premium to NAV of approximately 2.05%. For reference, CIK has traded at an average discount to NAV of 2.03% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current premium pricing in relation to CIK's historical range, I added the red line to the graph below. As we can see, CIK now sits around one of the highest premium price-to-NAV valuations over the last decade, which makes it hard to recommend accumulating shares.

To get better clarity on the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.12 per share, while CIK experienced a net realized loss of about $0.01 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of 2025, CIK's net investment income levels are aligned to generate similar income to its previous years. This consistent range of net investment income indicates that the fund's strategy of generating income from securities is highly efficient. However, the report indicates that net investment income levels aren't entirely capable of supporting distributions on their own.

Therefore, CIK is reliant on positive net realized gains to make up the difference in earnings. The only problem with this is that net realized gains are dependent on positive market momentum so that the fund can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. So as you can imagine, it's difficult for CIK to capture positive net realized gains during periods where market indexes are declining. For instance, this is why CIK experienced a large net realized loss of $0.66 per share during 2022's market decline. This reliance on net realized gains has slowed the rate of NAV growth over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $3.42 per share
  • 2021: $3.43 per share
  • 2022: $2.73 per share
  • 2023: $2.96 per share
  • 2024: $2.97 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $2.94 per share

The Fed recently initiated a 25 basis point cut to interest rates this week. Since the fund has exposure to floating-rate securities, I believe that a lower interest rate will serve as a growth catalyst. As we can see below, CIK's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near-zero levels. Conversely, CIK's share price has struggled to regain positive momentum back above its pre-pandemic levels since interest rates were raised to their decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023.

Risk Profile

As part of the fund's operating strategy, CIK utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 28.18% of assets, which I consider to be an aggressive use of leverage during this higher interest rate environment. While leverage can amplify earnings, investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. Additionally, a higher interest rate environment means that the fund's operating spread between the cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner, which makes it harder to grow the fund's assets.

Another source of risk would be the fund's exposure to below-investment-grade securities. For reference, securities with a credit rating below Baa are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on excessive levels of debt to fuel operations. The lower the credit rating, the higher the probability of CIK being impacted by defaults. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the level of leveraged loan defaults can increase as high as 3.5% by March of 2026, assuming slower economic growth and the impact of trade disruptions related to tariffs. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (A - Baa): 49.2%
  • Below Investment Grade (Ba - Caa): 35.83%
  • Not Rated: 14.96%

So in a scenario where future interest rate cuts happen slower than anticipated, it may take some time for CIK's share price to recover. Although the Fed did cut interest rates recently, there's still the risk that these smaller cuts will not have a meaningful impact on the fund's share price recovery. Therefore, investors need to consider the risk that CIK may continue to trade sideways for the remainder of the year.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest monthly distribution of $0.02 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9%. According to the prior years listed on the semi-annual report, CIK has paid out an annual distribution totaling $0.27 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, CIK has demonstrated that it is capable of generating earnings in excess of this payout amount. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.21 per share
  • 2021: $0.28 per share
  • 2022: ($0.43) per share
  • 2023: $0.50 per share
  • 2024: $0.28 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): 0.11 per share

As we can see below, CIK's earnings have allowed the fund to keep payouts very consistent over the last decade. This makes CIK a great choice for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income year after year. While the fund doesn't have a history of raising payouts, I still believe that this consistency should be commended. Consistency in payouts essentially enables investors to create their own dividend growth through reinvestment or with the contribution of additional capital over time.

Unfortunately, it seems like CIK does not prioritize paying out distributions using tax-efficient methods. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that the payout for August was made up of 80.9% net investment income and 19.1% return of capital. Distributions fueled by net investment income are generally classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in CIK may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on CIK at this time. Although the fund has an excellent history of paying out consistent distributions over the last decade, there aren't many excess earnings to contribute to any meaningful NAV growth. I would like to see the fund's reliance on positive net realized gains reduced, so that CIK can still experience NAV growth even if net realized gains are weak. Additionally, the fund now trades at one of the highest premium-to-NAV valuations over the last ten years, so I will remain on the sidelines and assess performance through a lower interest rate environment.

data.title: Acuren's Surge Does Not Mean It's Time To Pile In
data.description:
Lately, things have been very interesting for shareholders of Acuren Corporation (NYSE:TIC). The company, which has historically operated as a provider of critical asset integrity services, has seen a surge in share price as of late. Over the last five days, shares are up 26%. And over the last month, they are up 47.5%. A lot of this move higher comes after a couple of events, namely the release of its second quarter results for the 2025 fiscal year on August 14th and, just 10 days before that, the completion of its merger with NV5 Global in a $1.7 billion transaction that I wrote about at the time it was initially announced.

I wish I could say I understand exactly why the stock has risen. But honestly, there isn't much in the way of news here. Even the latest upgrade that I could find, which came from Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), came around three weeks ago. In all likelihood, this appreciation can be chalked up to optimism following the merger between Acuren and NV5 Global and the opportunities that offers investors moving forward. But honestly, based on the data that's provided, I really don't see significant upside justified from this point on. Because of this, I believe that maintaining the company as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right move.

Why I’m not bullish

In my last article about Acuren, published in May of this year, I talked about how it and NV5 Global had agreed to emerge in a cash and stock deal. This worked out to an effective purchase price at the time it was announced of $1.7 billion. And while there was some uncertainty about the transaction concluding because of a 60 day go-shop period that NV5 Global was able to utilize, the deal ultimately came to fruition. In that article, I talked about the benefits of the transaction and what it would mean for shareholders moving forward. This included the prospect of synergies that management pegged at around $20 million on an annual run rate basis.

On August 4th, the management team at Acuren completed the merger, creating what it called a market leading North American provider of testing, inspection, certification, and compliance services that also engages in engineering services. In the latest investor presentation that management provided, which covered for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, it was discussed just how large the combined business is. Total revenue in 2024 amounted to $2.04 billion. And with over 230 locations in operation, more than 11,000 employees, and with 30% of its revenue coming from international markets, it is truly a large and global firm.

In the image above, you can see how revenue for both companies have increased over the last several years. Even though there were certain weak spots, such as the global financial crisis that began in 2008 and the COVID-19 pandemic that started in late 2019, the overall trend for the firms has been positive. This is especially true when it comes to the operations of NV5 Global.

Normally, I would like to dig into recent quarterly results. And those did come out on August 14th. But all of that data was before the merger was completed. Honestly, I think that going over those details would be a disservice to you since Acuren is no longer the company that it was at that time. It is now significantly larger as we have already talked about.

While the synergies are interesting and have the potential to create a lot of value for shareholders, one thing that I am particularly interested in would be the cross-selling opportunities that exist. Management provided, in the image above, multiple examples of these opportunities. They include, for instance, offering inspection and mitigation services to bridges, tunnels, railway systems, and water systems, all as bundled solutions that cater to existing clients. They also include lab testing and engineering integration, where services like failure analysis, material selection, welding qualification, and corrosion analysis are all bundled. There are opportunities that exist in other areas as well like energy transition, geospatial, and so much more. And this even extends into geographic expansion, with Canada being a notable target.

In my previous article about Acuren and NV5 Global, I talked about how the combined company should look. I figured, for instance, that adjusted operating cash flow, which is operating cash flow without changes in working capital factored in, would come in at around $141.3 million. This was based on figures from 2024. And according to management's own figures, EBITDA would come in at around $330.2 million. Management remains confident in the prospect of synergies, highlighting that $20 million in annual run rate savings would be possible. This would push EBITDA up to around $350.2 million, while adjusted operating cash flow would rise to around $161.3 million.

This year, things are looking rather interesting. For the first half of 2025, Acuren on its own generated adjusted operating cash flow of around $31.5 million. That's down from the $55.4 million reported at the same time one year earlier. It also was responsible for EBITDA of about $80.4 million. That compared unfavorably to the $94.6 million reported the previous year. On the other hand, NV5 Global was more of a mixed bag. While its adjusted operating cash flow contracted from $45.7 million to $43.8 million, its EBITDA grew from $51.8 million to $58 million.

If we assume that the rest of this year will look like the first half of the year did on a combined basis, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $118 million and EBITDA of $292.3 million. I am always skeptical of synergies. Companies all too often overpromise and underdeliver. However, in its second-quarter earnings call, the management team at Acuren claimed that they believe that their synergy estimates are conservative. They specified that they anticipate growing that figure over the next several months and that investors will be updated on their progress. In fact, the company even established an integration management office that is working on achieving fast wins while also focusing on revenue growth opportunities. So if we assume that synergies do come to fruition, then adjusted operating cash flow would be around $138 million, while EBITDA would be about $312.3 million on a pro forma basis.

One thing that I would like to point out as well is that there have been some changes here. One of these is a positive in terms of evaluating the company, while another one is a negative. In my previous article about the firm, I used management’s estimate for net debt of $1.53 billion. That is what they estimated would be the net debt of the combined business, which included $850 million of new debt being added on to the $771 million of debt that Acuren boasted on a standalone basis. But if we use results as of the end of the most recent quarter for each firm, we would get net debt of $1.47 billion. So that works out in our favor since it means the firm is less leveraged than anticipated. On the other hand, shares have risen quite a bit, as I pointed out already. That makes further upside more difficult.

All told, we have a company that has a market capitalization as of this writing of about $2.81 billion. This would work out to an enterprise value of approximately $4.28 billion. If we use these figures and we use the historical results from 2024 and the estimates for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the combined business are valued on a pro forma basis. These aren't bad multiples, but they aren't great. Even if the planned synergies come to fruition, this is not a value play by any means. Relative to other similar firms, however, the stock doesn't look expensive either. In the table below, I compared it to five other similar enterprises. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, four of the five firms were cheaper than it is. But on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, two of the five were cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Acuren Corporation 23.8 14.6
Huron Consulting Group (HURN) 13.1 16.0
Innodata (INOD) 55.5 46.8
LegalZoom.com (LZ) 11.9 25.7
ICF International (ICFI) 12.7 10.9
Clarivate (CLVT) 4.6 7.4

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I can understand why some investors should be optimistic. The upgrade that the stock got a few weeks ago, combined with management's rhetoric regarding larger-than-expected synergies and the prospect of robust cross selling, all look bullish for the firm. But because of how the stock is currently priced, I don't really see this as an opportunity that will continue to outperform the broader market. Because of this, I believe that maintaining Acuren as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right move.

data.title: International Consolidated Airlines Has Earned Its Share Price, But Leaves A Lot To Be Desired
data.description:
International Consolidated Airlines Group aka International Airlines Group [IAG] (OTCPK:BABWF) (OTCPK:ICAGY) has recently become a staple in the "FTSE rocket stocks", due to its unprecedented growth and momentum after the travel industry recovered from COVID. But in recent months, it seems to have been quite stagnant. This can be attributed to many external factors, but how does it look internally? We have to know how healthy the company is to write off any market fears to geopolitics, especially with airlines. Let's start with their most recent report:

H1 2025

Highlights

• Revenue growth in the first half of 8.0% to €15,906 million, reflecting strong demand for our network and brands

• Operating profit before exceptional items in H1 2025 increased by 43.5% to €1,878 million, driven by revenue, fuel and foreign exchange benefits

• Q2 operating profit before exceptional items increased by 35.4% to €1,680 million

• Transformation programme delivering a better customer experience and supported a 2.9 pts increase in operating margin to 11.8%in the first half

• Strong balance sheet gives flexibility in capital allocation: net leverage of 0.7x and gross leverage of 2.0x, supported by significant free cash flow

• Adjusted Earnings per share growth of 69.9%

• €1.5 billion of cash returns to shareholders in 2025 through dividends and buybacks, announced to date

These highlights are a major boost to keep IAG on its record of beating expectations; an EPS boost of ~70%, a major share buyback and dividends programme, but a revenue boost of only 8%, which, compared to its 5YR revenue performance, leaves a lot on the table. But that is just superficial for airlines; we need to look at IAG's operational performance to gauge their true position in the global market.

Further in the report, IAG reports their ASKs (Available seat kilometers), which is how many seats travelled the equivalent of a kilometer. (1 seat travelling 2 kilometers is 2 ASKs). We can then ascertain the demand for IAG's airlines by taking the revenue and dividing it by ASKs to find the RASK in euros.

15,906 / 170,050 = €0.09 = $0.11

We can convert this to revenue per available tonne kilometers (RATK) by representing each occupied seat as 90kg (by using PLF to find the number of seats occupied), and adding the cargo tonne kilometers:

15906 / ((170,050 * 0.841) / 0.090) + 2596) = €0.098 = $0.12

As a reference, the average RATK for airlines in Q2 2025, according to my previous articles, was calculated at $0.1265. This puts IAG as pretty average globally.

However, one big advantage IAG has is that this half, fuel costs per ASK have dropped by 10% compared to H1 2024, which contributed to their EPS rising by 70%. We can see that as follows:

Fuel costs per ASK H12025 (€): 0.0207

Fuel costs per ASK H12024 (€): 0.0230

0.0230-0.0207 = 0.0023

0.0023 * 170,050,000,000 = €391,115,000

With a profit after tax of €1,301,000,000, this accounts for a significant margin of this. If fuel costs were to stay the same, there would have been an increase of only 0.55%, not the 43.8% rise we saw YoY. The staggering CapEx of €3,000,000,000 was only possible due to this, and the €1,500,000,000 payout to investors over the course of the next year is going to be hinged on fuel prices not increasing, or being well hedged, barring increasing debt to pay out to investors.

IATA Report, July 2025

Of course, to predict the state of the stock, we have to look at how European airlines are doing on the global market at large, which will influence the routes that IAG profits from, seeing as all of their airlines are based in Europe.

There are 3 main graphs we should look at:

The first of these shows that global RPK (revenue passenger kilometer) growth has rebounded from June after a rocky start to the summer. North America seems to be slowly recovering internationally, and Europe continues to play second fiddle to Asia Pacific. However, this is still promising as European international flights still contributed to 20% of Global RPK this July. This still consolidates Europe as the global flight hub it aims to be, as many of the international routes flown in Asia Pacific are domestic to the region itself.

This is where we can see that growth is still increasing in speed in Europe this month, after June, as is the case in most of the world, except for Latin America and the Caribbean.

And as outlook goes, I always like to take the IATA scheduled flights year over year as a litmus test of the next few months for airline travel. As we can see YoY, bookings are still growing. It seems travel dips seem to last no longer than the bookings for a quarter, as the "Liberation Day" travel worries seem a distant memory.

I would recommend you read the full report here, as it provides a lot of interesting and important trends.

The Underlying Financials

Of course, to form an opinion, we have to look at the data. Starting with value ratios, taken from Seeking Alpha, as follows.

Metric GAAP Non-GAAP Industry GAAP Industry non-GAAP
PE 7.55 6.79 23.11 20.77
PEG 0.24 0.3 1.17 1.84
PS 0.64 0.61 1.69 1.67

We should then calculate the cost of equity (which is the performance over a year that the stock has to match to justify the risk of owning the stock compared to federal bonds)

Beta is taken at 1.96 from Yahoo, and market YoY growth is taken from the annual return from the annualised return of 2014-2024 adjusted for inflation. Let's calculate the cost needed to justify owning this stock:

ERP = Predicted market YoY growth - Current 5Y federal bond yield = 8.3% - 4% = 4.3%.

Cost of Equity = Current federal bond yield + (Beta x ERP) = 4% + (1.96 x 4.3%) = 12.43%

We can use this as our discount rate to perform a basic 5-year 2-stage DCF model with Seeking Alpha Estimates, varying the model and terminal growth to obtain a range of upsides from the current share price.

Revenue: Projection of Revenue using Seeking Alpha estimates at a 5YR CAGR of 15.36%.

EBIT: Earnings Before Tax projection using current EBIT * Revenue Growth Rate for the given year using Seeking Alpha estimates

Tax: Taken from the mean average effective tax rates over the last 5 years (a 5YR CAGR of 20.4%), then applying this tax rate to the Revenue for the given year from Seeking Alpha estimates.

NOPAT: Net Operating Profit After Tax is calculated using EBIT - Tax.

Depreciation: Taken from a 5-year average using Seeking Alpha estimates. (5YR CAGR of 6.73%)

Capex: Capital Expenditures taken from Analyst predictions, aligned with Seeking Alpha revenue CAGR post 2028.

Change in WC: Change in Working Capital is taken from the Current Working Capital multiplied by the Average Change in Working Capital over the last 5 years, then applying the same average change YoY at a CAGR of 21.5%.

FCFF: Discounted Free Cash Flow to Firm = (NOPAT + Depreciation - Capex - Change in WC) / ((1 + CoE) ^ (Years since 2024))

FCFF w/out Capex: Also calculated and used as an example of the business's underlying cash flow prior to long-term investments

During stage 1 of the model, called the growth stage, discounted FCFF is calculated, alongside discounted FCFF w/out Capex, for each year.

Note: values have been rounded up to the nearest million for display purposes only; calculations have been performed pre-rounding.

STAGE 1 FY2024 FY2025 FY2026 FY2027 FY2028 FY2029
Revenue (€MM) 32100 33282 34636 35998 36260 37030
EBIT (€MM) 3563 4338 4461 4680 5399 6228
Tax (€MM) 727 885 910 955 1102 1271
NOPAT (€MM) 2836 3453 3551 3725 4297 4957
Deprec (€MM) 2026 2162 2307 2462 2628 2805
CAPEX (€MM) 3058 3868 4005 4250 4903 5656
WC (€MM) 84 102 124 151 183 222
FCFF (€MM) 1720 1463 1368 1257 1151 1049
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 4778 4904 4536 4248 4220 4198

We then perform stage 2. This is called the terminal stage and takes the final FCFF and applies a terminal growth rate to the discount to find the value of the firm after the scope of the simulation. We will vary the terminal growth between -3% and 3% (which are my standard worst-best-case scenarios) to provide us with a reasonable spread of outcomes to use as a dataset in our models.

STAGE 2 -3% 0% 3%
FCFF (€MM) 3785 4698 6193
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 15147 18803 24786

Finally, we can obtain our valuation. The final intrinsic value comes from the sum of the Discounted FCFFs and the Terminal Growth FCFF, divided by the number of outstanding shares (4.917 billion).

Terminal Growth Rate -3% 0% 3%
FCFF per share (€) 2.33 2.52 2.85
FCFF w/out CAPEX per share (€) 8.19 8.98 10.28

This shows that, based on fundamentals, the share price is quite high, almost twice the expected amount; however, it must be mentioned that with airlines, CapEx is almost always a long-term investment in aircraft. In this case, IAG has bought over 50 aircraft in the last year. So we will include both sets of data for mitigating the investment effects on cash flows.

We can map each of the upsides or downsides from the current share price (€4.59) on a graph, as a coefficient (from 1 for clarity of positives vs negatives) of the current share price.

As we can see, the value ratios from earlier dominate this graph with over 5x the current share price for the PEG compared to the Industry average. However, this can be attributed to earnings growth due to fluctuating fuel prices. Then again, airline travel is still growing, so are the pessimistic FCFF predictions realistic? So to really balance the books, we are going to have to run a Monte Carlo simulation.

We will fluctuate each input coefficient randomly (Log-Normal) from the above graph by +/- σ (the standard deviation) and plot each prediction's chance of exceeding the cost of equity by mapping it against a Log-Normal curve formed of all the predictions. We are using the "Log-Normal" curve because securities and stocks are often fat-tailed statistically. The graph plotted also contains a "Target Price" which is ascertained by multiplying the coefficient picked by the current share price.

A trendline is then formed to show the relationship between the P(x>CoE) and the target share price.

The output is very promising; we have a large range of results, which align with the fact that the FCFF is pessimistic and the PEG is optimistic. We also have an R^2 value of 0.8382, which is quite strong for our cubic trendline. (Which passes the common-sense test as the higher the target share price, the more likely it is to outperform 12.43% gains).

To find a reasonable rating, we will take the mean from the P(x>CoE) axis, and then use the trendline to find the Target Price at said value. As there is an asymptote at 1, this should mitigate the Target Price being skewed by overly optimistic predictions derived from PEG.

Our average P(x>CoE) is 0.8143, which is an 81.43% chance of exceeding the Cost of Equity. To obtain our rating, we can adapt and plot this against an empirical ratings curve:

Rating y = |(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ
Strong Sell >7
Sell 5 < y < 7
Hold 3 < y < 5
Buy 1 < y < 3
Strong Buy <1

|(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ = 1.2696; therefore, as 1 < 1.2696< 3, IAG secures just a "Buy" rating. This downgrade could be due to the increased volatility it has experienced over the past month, affecting its rating due to geopolitical influence and oil prices, alongside major CapEx and cash flow differences from your normal industrial. This analysis really reflects the state of airline stocks, as a simple look at the P/E might be misleading, and does not paint the full picture, including risk and volatility, of owning such a stock, despite, in this case, the excellent financial report for H1 2025.

The price target at this average can be calculated using the equation of the trendline:

y = 138.14x^3 - 296.33x^2 + 226.26x - 51.925

PT = 74.59 - 196.49 + 184.244 - 51.925 = €10.42

Which, when rounded to the nearest 10c gives us a PT of €10.40, which translates to $12.20 for BABWF, and $24.40 for ICAGY.

In Conclusion

A tumultuous first half for the airline industry has seemingly seen IAG pull the rabbit out of the hat and produce a stellar H1 2025, with excellent investor returns and a good outlook. However, the cynic would ask if the fact that their earnings performance hinges on global fuel prices, and travel restrictions being mitigated by careful route planning, would one slip-up spoil the party?

This has obviously been reflected in the underlying financials as a major CapEx commitment and a seemingly curbed revenue growth rate in future years, downgrading IAG to "Buy" with a PT of $12.20. Still a lot to be earned, with good payout to investors, but only those with an appetite for geopolitics.

data.title: Alibaba: This Rally Has Legs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Earlier this year, I wrote an article on Alibaba (NYSE:BABA)(OTCPK:BABAF), the giant Chinese e-commerce and technology company, and how I considered it a “Strong Buy.” My optimistic call has aged nicely: since mid-May, the stock has returned around 23%, even beating the U.S. S&P 500 index by a considerable margin. Those investors who followed my advice will be enjoying a clear win.

Here’s my update with fresh numbers and a closer look at what’s next for this company. The core idea is simple: Alibaba's share price is growing aggressively again as its AI operations are taking off in earnest. The company also benefits from a plump cash cushion to fund future growth. Meanwhile, the stock is still far cheaper than U.S. tech giants like Amazon. Add this all together, and my bullish stance still stands as a "Strong Buy."

Recent Developments

Alibaba’s quarterly results were a bit of a mixed bag. The company fell slightly short of Wall Street predictions on both revenue and profit—what analysts call a dual miss. Overall sales rose only 2.3% compared to the same quarter last year. Pretty modest for a tech giant.

Nevertheless, those numbers hid an important positive: AI and cloud computing. The company’s Cloud Intelligence division generated an impressive $4.6 billion in revenue, up a strong 26% YoY. And then there are the AI-related products, which jumped by triple-digit percentages for the eighth consecutive quarter, a sign that demand for Alibaba’s AI tools is growing at lightning speed—and consistently too. This success proves that Alibaba is much more than just an e-commerce giant. It is fast becoming a key player in the global AI race. By the way, analysts from Morgan Stanley forecast that the Chinese AI industry will be worth $1.4 trillion by 2030. In addition, analysts from Goldman Sachs forecast generative AI is expected to add between 0.2% and 0.3% to China’s GDP by 2030—a breathtaking figure for an economy of China’s size.

And Alibaba is ready to benefit from this upward trend. Its Alibaba Cloud is already China’s largest cloud provider, with the chips adding that competitive edge. It’s not going unnoticed either. Barclays recently commented that “Alibaba cloud growth is likely to accelerate” thanks to the AI momentum and booming customer demand.

Worth mentioning is that Alibaba is also designing its own AI chips to train large language models. This will reduce its dependency on pricey hardware from companies like Nvidia (NVDA). Home-grown chips could not only cut costs but give the company greater control over its technology.

Furthermore, Alibaba already sells its AI chips to large domestic customers, which can potentially become another massive revenue stream. This represents a huge potential opportunity for Alibaba, as evidenced by Nvidia's $17 billion in revenue from China in FY2025. I also want to emphasize that Nvidia's China revenue growth has been absolutely staggering over the last three years, soaring from below $6 billion to $17 billion.

One reason Alibaba can chase these opportunities is its solid balance sheet. The company is sitting on an impressive pile of cash, and continues to generate more through its core business, allowing it to invest aggressively in its next generation of technology. Last quarter, Alibaba invested net $5.4 billion in CapEx, building and upgrading its physical assets like data centers and networks. Much of the spending also went on its cloud infrastructure and 5G network backbones. All of this investment is critical to ensure that the AI applications have the necessary computing power and lightning-speed connections.

After years of weak consumer spending in China, the tide is finally turning. Consulting firm McKinsey reports that retail sales across China grew 5% YoY in the first half of 2025, exceeding expectations. As China's leading e-commerce player, Alibaba will inevitably benefit from this positive shift in the macro environment.

Growth is particularly strong in China’s lower-tier cities, where many households are shopping online for essentials. And this is a trend that directly benefits Alibaba since it runs one of the country’s largest online platforms. Finally, Alibaba’s international commerce group is poised to exploit a decent share of that growth as global shoppers seek affordable Chinese goods.

Valuation Update

Alibaba is often referenced as the “Amazon of China,” and with good reason. Like Amazon, the company dominates e-commerce at home and operates a leading cloud platform. However, Alibaba trades at much lower valuation ratios than Amazon—across every metric. Some of this is due to the China factor, since U.S. stocks usually command higher premiums than Chinese ones. But the gap is so wide that it cannot be explained by geopolitics alone.

Looking ahead, the company’s EPS is set to keep growing thanks to its extensive AI initiatives in the pipeline. And if profits rise as expected, the company’s price-to-earnings ratio should naturally shrink, making stock appear even cheaper compared to its earnings power. And it’s this combination of strong growth and low valuation that is rare enough to make me sit up and take notice.

Risks Update

Of course, no investment is without its risks, and despite all the positives, Alibaba is no exception.

First up is the politics and geopolitics: The Chinese government continues to monitor its tech sector closely, and regulatory crackdowns, new rules, and unexpected audits can impact stock prices. And let’s not forget that relations between China and the U.S. remain tense. Some trade deals are still only temporary and could unravel completely.

Next, let’s look at the competition. The company is surrounded by fierce rivals in every possible segment, from companies like PDD Holdings and JD.com, who are also fighting for a piece of the e-commerce pie. In cloud computing, it’s local giants like Huawei and Tencent who are closing the gap with their aggressive investment plans.

Finally, the above chart shows how Alibaba's share price is often weaker towards the end of the calendar year. While seasonality is not a major risk, it can still influence short-term investor sentiment. So, I advise any investors to weigh up these risks before buying.

Bottom Line

There’s no doubt that Alibaba is a major player in the global tech industry. Its core e-commerce business is expanding rapidly as Chinese consumers are spending again. Its cloud computing and AI tools are growing at double- and triple-digit rates, while the company is investing heavily in infrastructure and competitive-edge AI chips. With a rock-solid balance sheet to boot, this is a company with funds for growth and innovation, and I can see it meeting its ambitious targets for both this year and 2023. Even so, we shouldn’t forget that the stock still trades at valuations far below those of the U.S., like Amazon, and as AI adoption continues to grow, this gap could narrow.

Taking into account the above mix of strong growth, cheap valuation, and booming trends in AI and e-commerce, I believe that investors willing to accept the political and competitive risks will benefit. I therefore stand by my call of a "strong buy" rating.
